title: Money growth and inflation
description: Practice what you know about the equation of exchange, the quantity theory of money, and the relationship between the money supply and inflation in this exercise.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to monetarists, what is the primary cause of inflation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The money supply increases faster than real output increases
The money supply decreases too rapidly for a long time
Any increase in the money supply
A stable velocity of money
A rapidly changing velocity of money

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following ideas is the quantity theory of money used to demonstrate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Inflation occurs because the money supply increases faster than real output increases
Deflation occurs when the money supply increases faster than real output increases
Real variables can be influenced by monetary policy in the long run
Changes in the velocity of money affect real variables
Real output never changes

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to monetarist theory, what happens if the money supply increases more rapidly than real output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Inflation occurs.
Deflation occurs.
The velocity of money increases.
The velocity of money decreases.
Nominal output decreases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the primary cause of deflation according to monetarist theory?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The money supply decreases faster than real output decreases.
The money supply increases too rapidly for a long time.
Any decrease in the money supply.
A stable quantity velocity of money
A rapidly changing velocity of money

assessment_items.question_content:
Assume that the velocity of money in Theopolis is 3 and the aggregate supply curve is vertical at $\$100$ million. 

**What impact will a $10\%$ increase in the money supply have on real output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There will be no impact on real output
Real output will increase by $30\%$
Real output will increase by $10\%$
Real output will increase by $3\%$
Real output will increase by $\dfrac{3}{10\%}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Assume that wages and prices are fully flexible and all inflation is correctly anticipated. 

**According to the quantity theory of money, what would be the impact of expansionary monetary policy on real output and the price level?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: No impact on real output, price level increases
No impact on real output, no impact on the price level
Real output increases, price level increases
Real output increases, no impact on price level
Real output decreases, price level decreases

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to the quantity theory of money, what happens if the money supply increases by 10% if the current rate of unemployment is equal to the natural rate of unemployment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The price level increases by 10%
Real output increases 10%
The price level increases by 5% and real output increases by 5%
Real output increases more than the increase in the price level
Real output and the velocity of money both increase

assessment_items.question_content:
Nominal GDP is $\$100$ million and the money supply is $\$25$ million.

**According to the quantity theory of money, what is the velocity of money?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The quantity theory of money states that :

$M\times V=P \times Y$

And the variable V is constant.
$P\times Y$ is the price level times real GDP. In other words, 

$\begin{align} P\times Y&=\text{ nominal GDP}\\\\
M\times V& =P\times Y\\\\
M\times V& =\$100\text{ million}\end{align}$
M is the money supply, so 

$\begin{align}M\times V&=P\times Y\\\\
\$25 \text{ million} \times  V &= \$100 \text{ million}\end{align}$
Solving for the velocity of money:

$\begin{align} M\times V &= P\times Y\\\\
\$25\text{ million} \times  V &= \$100 million\\\\
V&=4\end{align}$

choices.content: $\$125$ million

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to the quantity theory of money, which of the following best describes what determines the rate of inflation in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The rate of growth of the money supply
The rate of change of the velocity of money
The growth of real GDP per capita
The growth of the price level
The rate of inflation is constant in the long run

assessment_items.question_content:
The economy of Hamsterville currently produces a combination of goods indicated by point X in the graph shown here.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a11fc74da062771653506beb5c44b34387ede34d)

**Based on this graph, what does monetarism say is the effect of a central bank purchase of bonds on real output in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: No change in real output in the long run; price level increases
Real output increases in the long run; price level increases
Real output decreases in the long run; price level increases
No change in real output in the long run; price level decreases
No change in real output or the price level

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to the quantity theory of money, changes in which variable(s) from the equation of exchange determine(s) the rate of inflation in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: M only
P only
V only
Y only
Y and V

assessment_items.question_content:
The economy of Burginville has experienced high rates of inflation over the past 10 years. 

**According to the quantity theory of money, which of the following is the most likely explanation for the sustained inflation during this period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Expansionary monetary policy
Contractionary monetary policy
A money supply that has increased at the same pace as real output
Better banking technology that has decreased the velocity of money
Strong economic growth
